frontmatter.title: If and Switch Statements in JavaScript

children.value: Programs do not become useful because they can store data or repeat actions. Programs become useful because they can make decisions. A payroll program must decide whether to apply overtime. A grading system must decide which letter grade to assign. A menu-driven application must decide which operation to run based on the user’s choice. In every case, the program examines a condition and then follows one path instead of another.
to perform this kind of decision-making. Two of the most important selection structures are the
statement. These statements allow a program to evaluate expressions, compare values, and choose among alternative blocks of code.

as simple syntax features. That view is too shallow. These statements express program logic. They shape control flow, determine which code runs, and affect correctness, readability, and maintainability. Poorly written decision logic creates bugs that hide in plain sight. Clear decision logic makes programs easier to test, debug, and extend.

statements work in JavaScript, why they matter, how JavaScript evaluates conditions, and when one structure fits better than the other. It also addresses execution order, truthy and falsy values, common mistakes, and practical coding strategies.
This chapter explains how JavaScript uses

to control decision-making. It distinguishes syntax from behavior, shows how JavaScript evaluates conditions, and demonstrates how selection statements influence execution order. It also examines common logic errors, compares
, and presents best practices for writing clear and reliable branching code.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:
Explain the purpose of selection statements in JavaScript.
Predict execution order in branching code.
Distinguish between Boolean expressions and general expressions used as conditions.
Explain how truthy and falsy values affect
Identify common mistakes involving comparison operators, block structure, and fall-through behavior.
Debug branching errors in beginner-level JavaScript programs.

Selection statements allow a program to choose among alternative actions. Instead of executing every statement in a straight line, the program tests a condition and then decides which block of code to run.
In JavaScript, the two main selection mechanisms are:
statements, which evaluate one or more conditions in sequence
statements, which compare one expression against several possible values
A selection statement does not repeat code. It does not store data. It directs
Control flow determines the order in which a program executes statements. Selection statements change that order by allowing some statements to run and others to be skipped.
is a control structure that chooses one path of execution from multiple possibilities based on a condition or expression.
Without decision-making, a program behaves like a train on a single fixed track. Real software cannot work that way. Programs must respond to input, state, and context.
A JavaScript program may need to:
check whether a password is correct
determine whether a number is positive, negative, or zero
apply discounts based on purchase amount
display different messages based on user role
validate form data before submitting it
Every one of these tasks depends on branching logic.
Poor decision-making code causes real problems. A missing
can trigger the wrong operation. A badly ordered
chain can make later conditions unreachable. A misunderstood truthy value can make a program behave as though data exists when it does not.
Good selection logic is not decoration. It is structural engineering.

statement is the most flexible decision-making structure in JavaScript. It evaluates a condition. If that condition is true, JavaScript executes the associated block. If the condition is false, JavaScript skips that block.
The condition appears inside parentheses. The body appears inside braces.

It continues with the next statement after the block.
That execution order matters. JavaScript does not “look ahead” and run both branches. It evaluates the condition first, then chooses.

When a program must choose from more than two possibilities, JavaScript can test conditions in sequence.
JavaScript evaluates these conditions from top to bottom.
Skip the rest of the chain
Once one condition succeeds, JavaScript stops testing the remaining
chain. If you place broader conditions before narrower ones, you can accidentally block later cases.

This logic is flawed. It works syntactically but fails conceptually.
6. Boolean Expressions, Truthy Values, and Falsy Values

children.label: 6. Boolean Expressions, Truthy Values, and Falsy Values

children.value: . That is incomplete. JavaScript allows any expression in a condition. It then converts that expression to a Boolean value.
is an expression that evaluates to either
JavaScript also allows non-Boolean values in conditions. It converts them internally.
contains an empty string, JavaScript treats it as falsy.
, it performs an internal Boolean conversion. It does not require the programmer to write:
but that is effectively the idea.
Truthy and falsy behavior makes code compact, but it also creates traps. A value of
may be a valid numeric input, but JavaScript treats it as falsy. If you write code carelessly, you may reject valid data by mistake.
That is not JavaScript being broken. That is the programmer choosing the wrong test.
7. Comparison and Logical Operators in Conditions

children.label: 7. Comparison and Logical Operators in Conditions

children.value: Selection statements rely heavily on operators that build conditions.

>   greater than
<   less than
>=  greater than or equal to
<=  less than or equal to
=== strictly equal to
!== strictly not equal to
are usually the correct equality operators to use.

&&  logical AND
||  logical OR
!   logical NOT

statements can express layered decision-making, but they can also become messy fast. If nesting becomes deep, the code often needs redesign. Sometimes a compound condition or a different program structure reads more clearly.
statement provides another way to choose among multiple paths. It works best when one expression can take several discrete values.

children.label: 12. When to Use if Versus switch

children.value: Students often ask which statement is “better.” That question is sloppy. The right question is which statement fits the problem.
you need compound logic such as
comparisons are not based on a single expression
one expression is compared against several exact values
the alternatives are discrete and fixed
readability improves through a clean menu-like structure
This works naturally because the logic depends on ranges.
This is cleaner than a long chain of exact-value comparisons.

This example shows an important design lesson: the two statements are not rivals. They solve different kinds of branching problems.
Common Mistake: Forgetting Braces in Multi-Line Blocks

children.label: Common Mistake: Forgetting Braces in Multi-Line Blocks

children.value: Only the first statement belongs to the
Even when braces are technically optional for one statement, using them consistently prevents stupid bugs. This is not a style nicety. It is defensive programming.
The second condition is unreachable for any score of 90 or above.

children.label: Common Mistake: Forgetting break in switch

children.value: When branching code behaves strangely, inspect three things first:
the order of conditions or cases
Most beginner errors in selection logic come from one of those three causes.
Write conditions that clearly express intent. A program should not feel like a riddle.
unless you have a specific reason not to. Loose equality introduces type coercion, and type coercion is often where clean logic goes to die.
conditions from most specific to most general when overlap exists.
Use braces consistently, even for single-statement blocks. Consistency prevents mistakes and improves readability.
for fixed exact-value branching, not for range testing. Forcing
when practical. Unexpected input exists. Pretending otherwise is not realism; it is negligence.
Keep branching logic as simple as possible. If a condition becomes hard to read, break it into smaller named variables.
A good selection statement does more than run correct code. It makes the program’s logic visible. When someone reads your
, they should see the decision structure immediately, not excavate it like an archaeological site.

statements allow JavaScript programs to make decisions and control execution flow. The
statement works well for flexible conditions, range checks, compound logic, and ordered tests. The
structure creates two-way branching, while the
chain supports multiple alternatives. JavaScript evaluates these conditions in order and stops once it finds a successful branch.
statement works best when one expression must be compared to several exact values. Each
handles unmatched values. Because JavaScript compares

This chapter also emphasized that syntax alone is not enough. Correct branching depends on execution order, operator choice, condition design, and awareness of truthy and falsy values. Clear selection logic improves correctness, readability, and debugging.
